grammar_of_verbs.description: dplyr implements a consistent set of verbs for data manipulation that combine naturally via the pipe operator
core_verbs: mutate() - add new variables as functions of existing variables
summarise() - collapse many values to summary
grammar_of_verbs.pipe_philosophy: The %>% operator enables left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading by piping the result from one step into the next. x %>% f(y) becomes f(x, y)
design_principles: Table-in, table-out consistency makes chaining easy
Functions take data frame as first parameter
Data masking allows using variables without df$ prefix
Functional approach with no side effects
grammar_of_verbs.citation: [1] Hadley Wickham et al. 'A Grammar of Data Manipulation • dplyr.' tidyverse.org. https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/

parsnip.purpose: Unified interface to models - try different models without syntactical minutiae
parsnip.modular_design: parsnip only solves interface issue, other packages handle preprocessing/tuning/metrics
parsnip.citation: [2] Max Kuhn. 'A Common API to Modeling and Analysis Functions • parsnip.' tidymodels.org. https://parsnip.tidymodels.org/

recipes.purpose: Preprocessing and feature engineering as composable steps
recipes.rationale: Recipes separated from models because different models often need different preprocessing
recipes.citation: [3] Julia Silge. 'Preprocess your data with recipes.' tidymodels.org. https://www.tidymodels.org/start/recipes/

workflows.purpose: Bundle preprocessing + model + postprocessing into unified object
workflows.benefits: Easier to train and test when recipe and model are paired
workflows.citation: [4] Davis Vaughan. 'Modeling Workflows • workflows.' tidymodels.org. https://workflows.tidymodels.org/

broom.purpose: Standardize model outputs into tidy tibbles
broom.consistency: Always returns tibbles, never rownames, predictable column names
broom.citation: [5] David Robinson. 'Convert Statistical Objects into Tidy Tibbles • broom.' tidymodels.org. https://broom.tidymodels.org/

core_functions.tq_get(): One-stop shop for web-based financial data in tidy format
core_functions.tq_mutate(): Add columns with financial function results
core_functions.tq_transmute(): Return new data frame (necessary for periodicity changes)

tidyquant_patterns.integrations: Wraps xts, zoo, quantmod, TTR, PerformanceAnalytics in tidy interface
tidyquant_patterns.philosophy: Few core functions with a lot of power
tidyquant_patterns.citation: [6] Matt Dancho. 'Tidy Quantitative Financial Analysis • tidyquant.' business-science.github.io. https://business-science.github.io/tidyquant/

python_signal_libraries.citation: [7] Quantopian. 'Zipline, a Pythonic Algorithmic Trading Library.' GitHub. https://github.com/quantopian/zipline

signal_definition_pattern.approach: Pipeline API for alpha factor computation
signal_definition_pattern.structure: Factors are transformations that output predictive signals
signal_definition_pattern.separation: Alpha factor computation separated from order execution
signal_definition_pattern.builtin_factors: Moving averages, Bollinger Bands, momentum indicators

methods: initialize() - called once at simulation start
handle_data() - called on each trading event
Data variable provides point-in-time current and historical data
architecture.lookahead_prevention: Automates algorithm reaction to trade events, ensures point-in-time data

python_signal_libraries.data_structures: Pandas DataFrames for input/output, PyData integration
key_features: Pipeline API for modular factor research

python_signal_libraries.citation: [8] Oleg Polakow. 'vectorbt: Find your trading edge.' GitHub/Documentation. https://github.com/polakowo/vectorbt

stats.performance: Numba-accelerated, tests thousands of strategies in seconds

signal_definition_pattern.structure: Signals generated through method chaining on indicators

architecture.core_concept: Each strategy instance in vectorized format, combined into multi-dimensional arrays

python_signal_libraries.data_structures: Multi-dimensional pandas DataFrames/Series for vectorized operations
key_features: MAE/MFE analysis for exit strategy optimization
limitations: Vectorized approach can be less intuitive for beginners
Less suited for complex state-dependent strategies

python_signal_libraries.citation: [9] mementum. 'backtrader: Python Backtesting library for trading strategies.' GitHub. https://github.com/mementum/backtrader

stats.used_by: Banks and trading houses for prototype testing

architecture.paradigm: Object-oriented with Strategy and Cerebro as core
architecture.cerebro: Controlling brain - handles strategy instantiation, optimization, execution
strategy_lifecycle: next() - trading logic on each bar

python_signal_libraries.data_structures: Custom data feeds, indicators return array-like objects

python_signal_libraries.citation: [10] Quantopian. 'alphalens: Performance analysis of predictive (alpha) stock factors.' GitHub. https://github.com/quantopian/alphalens

metrics: Returns Analysis - factor-sorted portfolio performance by quantile
Information Coefficient - correlation between factor and forward returns
Turnover Analysis - rebalancing frequency and costs

python_signal_libraries.key_metric: Information Ratio (mean(IC) / std(IC)) - rule of thumb: IR >= 0.05 is good
python_signal_libraries.integration: Works with Zipline for backtesting, Pyfolio for portfolio analysis

python_signal_libraries.citation: [11] Quantopian. 'pyfolio: Portfolio and risk analytics in Python.' GitHub. https://github.com/quantopian/pyfolio

tear_sheet_components: Performance statistics table - annual return, Sharpe, beta

architecture.output: Comprehensive tear sheets with plots and statistics

python_signal_libraries.citation: [12] Quantopian. 'empyrical: Common financial risk and performance metrics.' GitHub. https://github.com/quantopian/empyrical

architecture.input: Numpy arrays or Pandas Series of returns

python_signal_libraries.usage: Backend for pyfolio and zipline metric calculations

pairs_trading_framework.description: Statistical arbitrage strategy exploiting mean reversion in cointegrated asset pairs
key_concepts: Mean reversion - prices revert to long-term equilibrium
Cointegration - long-run stationary relationship between non-stationary series
Spread modeling - price difference exhibits mean-reverting behavior
pairs_trading_framework.citation: [13] Various authors. 'An Application of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process to Pairs Trading.' arXiv. https://arxiv.org/html/2412.12458v1

engle_granger_method.description: Two-step approach: OLS regression → residual stationarity test (ADF)
engle_granger_method.limitation: Sensitive to which asset is dependent variable, error accumulates between steps

johansen_test.description: Simultaneous estimation of cointegration relationship and testing
advantages: Finds hedge ratio and tests cointegration simultaneously
johansen_test.interpretation: Compare trace_stat and max_eig_stat against critical values

cointegration_testing.citation: [14] Stefan Jansen. 'Cointegration Tests & Pairs Trading.' Machine Learning for Trading. https://github.com/stefan-jansen/machine-learning-for-trading

ornstein_uhlenbeck_modeling.description: Continuous-time mean-reverting stochastic process for spread modeling
ornstein_uhlenbeck_modeling.process: dXt = θ(μ - Xt)dt + σdWt where θ is mean reversion speed
applications: Model price spread as OU process
Estimate parameters (θ, μ, σ) via MLE

optimal_stopping.approach: Maximize expected discounted value of trades
two_step_process: Fit OU process, find optimal asset ratio via max log-likelihood
Solve optimal stopping problem for entry/exit thresholds

ornstein_uhlenbeck_modeling.performance: Strategy with OU-based timing: 16.38% annual return, 1.34 Sharpe Ratio (Brazilian stocks)
ornstein_uhlenbeck_modeling.citation: [15] Hudson & Thames. 'Optimal Stopping in Pairs Trading: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Model.' https://hudsonthames.org/optimal-stopping-in-pairs-trading-ornstein-uhlenbeck-model/

convergence_trading.definition: Buy one asset forward, sell similar asset forward at higher price, profit on convergence
convergence_trading.commodities_example: Gold spot at $1000/oz, futures at $1200/oz → long spot, short futures
convergence_trading.calendar_spreads: Offsetting positions in same underlying, different expirations
risks: Convergence may not happen or take too long
Margin calls if prices diverge first
Negative skew in returns - consistent small profits, occasional large losses
convergence_trading.important_note: Commodities are typically trending markets, not mean-reverting - trend following works better

alfred_database.description: ArchivaL Federal Reserve Economic Data - vintage versions of economic data
alfred_database.purpose: Retrieve data as it was available on specific historical dates
use_cases: Reproduce past research with data available at the time
Build accurate forecasting models avoiding lookahead bias
Analyze policy decisions with contemporaneous data
alfred_database.bitemporal_modeling: Two timelines: observation date + revision date
alfred_database.python_access: fredapi package supports FRED and ALFRED
alfred_database.citation: [17] Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. 'Archival FRED | ALFRED.' https://alfred.stlouisfed.org/

ragged_edge_problem.description: Different publication lags create incomplete recent data (unbalanced dataset)
ragged_edge_problem.example: Some series current through this month, others only through last quarter
ragged_edge_problem.importance: Appropriately dealing with jagged edge is key for nowcasting
ragged_edge_problem.citation: [18] Multiple authors. 'Forecasting with real-time macroeconomic data: The ragged-edge problem.' ScienceDirect. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0164070411000346

data_revisions.challenge: First release is timely but noisy, gets revised as more info available
modeling_approaches: State-space models handle publication lags and revisions
Including simple revision model improves imputation accuracy
MIDAS (Mixed-frequency data sampling) handles unbalanced datasets
data_revisions.factor_midas: Nowcast low-frequency (GDP) using high-frequency indicators with ragged edge
data_revisions.citation: [19] European Central Bank. 'Factor MIDAS for Nowcasting and Forecasting with Ragged-Edge Data.' Oxford Bulletin. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1468-0084.2010.00591.x

lookahead_bias_prevention.definition: Using information not available at signal execution time
detection: No train/test split or walk-forward validation
prevention_techniques: Shift signals by one period: df['Position'] = df['Buy'].shift(1)
Event-driven backtesting (vs vectorized) - prevents indexing mistakes
Point-in-time databases track all historical revisions
lookahead_bias_prevention.citation: [20] Multiple sources. 'Look-Ahead Bias: What It Is & How to Avoid.' Analyzing Alpha. https://analyzingalpha.com/look-ahead-bias

xarray_library.description: N-dimensional labeled arrays for Python, inspired by pandas
advantages_over_pandas: Operations work regardless of dimension order
No need to reshape arrays for arithmetic
Better for ndim > 2 (panel data, climate data)

data_structures.DataArray: Single multi-dimensional array with labeled dimensions
data_structures.Dataset: Dict-like container of aligned DataArrays (like multi-dimensional DataFrame)

time_series_features: groupby() for split-apply-combine on any dimension
Integration with pandas for time indexing
xarray_library.use_cases: Geoscience, physics, machine learning, finance (multi-dimensional data)
xarray_library.citation: [21] xarray developers. 'xarray: N-D labeled Arrays and Datasets.' Journal of Open Research Software. https://openresearchsoftware.metajnl.com/articles/10.5334/jors.148

panel_data_approaches.definition: Data with multiple dimensions: entities × time × features

term_structure_modeling.description: Model forward curves as functions of time and maturity
term_structure_modeling.seasonality_decomposition: f(t) = s(t) + ε(t) where s is seasonal, ε is smooth deviation
term_structure_modeling.estimation: Kalman filtering for MLE of unobserved factors
python_tools: statsmodels - State space models for Kalman filtering
term_structure_modeling.r_package: NFCP - N-factor commodity pricing models with Kalman filtering
term_structure_modeling.citation: [22] Multiple authors. 'Modeling The Term Structure of Commodity Futures Curve.' Qeios. https://www.qeios.com/read/UUA0Q9

ranked_methods.purpose: Identify pairs with long-term stationary relationships
Test if spread/series is mean-reverting vs trending
ranked_methods.interpretation: Use both tests together (opposite null hypotheses)

ranked_methods.approach: Optimize on in-sample window, test on following out-of-sample period, roll forward
ranked_methods.benefit: Reduces overfitting, tests adaptability to changing markets
ranked_methods.use_case: Strategy validation and parameter stability testing

ranked_methods.purpose: Detect regime changes that invalidate historical relationships
ranked_methods.use_case: Identify when cointegration relationship breaks down

ranked_methods.purpose: Estimate mean reversion speed, equilibrium level, volatility
ranked_methods.estimation: Maximum Likelihood Estimation via Kalman filtering

ranked_methods.purpose: Measure correlation between signal and forward returns
ranked_methods.metric: Information Ratio (mean IC / std IC), benchmark: IR >= 0.05

ranked_methods.purpose: Model time-varying volatility for risk management
Measure how quickly spread reverts to mean
Estimate unobserved factors and time-varying parameters
ranked_methods.use_case: Term structure modeling, adaptive hedge ratios

ranked_methods.purpose: Separate seasonal patterns from trend and residual

citations: [23] statsmodels developers. 'Stationarity and detrending (ADF/KPSS).' statsmodels documentation. https://www.statsmodels.org/stable/examples/notebooks/generated/stationarity_detrending_adf_kpss.html
[24] Multiple authors. 'arch Python GARCH volatility modeling.' arch documentation. https://arch.readthedocs.io/

vintage_tenor_time_dimensions.problem: Handle data with vintage_date × observation_date × tenor × contracts
advantages: Operations work regardless of dimension order

tidy_format_for_signals.structure: Long format with columns: date, contract, signal_name, signal_value, metadata
advantages: Follows Hadley Wickham's tidy data principles
Easy to filter, group, and aggregate
tidy_format_for_signals.example: | date | contract | signal | value | confidence | metadata |

signal_specification_pattern.recommendation: Frozen dataclass similar to parsnip ModelSpec
benefits: Immutable - prevents side effects when reusing specs

fluent_interface_design.rationale: Method chaining improves readability and reduces intermediate variables
fluent_interface_design.pattern: Return self from methods to enable chaining
considerations: Avoid mutating self if immutability is desired
Don't make chains too long (readability suffers)
fluent_interface_design.citation: [26] Multiple authors. 'Fluent Interface in Python Using Method Chaining.' GitHub Gist. https://gist.github.com/thinkingserious/d1b06ee12a3613c0dc3b

benefits: Clean separation of interface and implementation
registry_pattern_for_extensibility.implementation: Global registry dict, get_signal(signal_type, engine) function

dual_path_architecture.rationale: Some signals need raw data access (e.g., for complex temporal patterns)
dual_path_architecture.pattern: Standard path (preprocess → fit → predict) vs Raw path (fit_raw → predict_raw)
example_use_cases: Standard: Linear signals with simple feature engineering
Raw: ARIMA with datetime handling, OU process fitting

what_tidy_signal_provides.gap: No unified grammar for signal detection
what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: Tidyverse-inspired verb API: detect_*, validate_*, evaluate_*
what_tidy_signal_provides.benefit: Consistent, composable interface across signal types

what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: Integrated cointegration → OU fitting → optimal stopping workflow
what_tidy_signal_provides.benefit: End-to-end pairs trading pipeline in one package

what_tidy_signal_provides.gap: No native support for vintage/point-in-time data
what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: First-class support for vintage_date dimension in data structures
what_tidy_signal_provides.benefit: Proper handling of data revisions, avoid lookahead bias

what_tidy_signal_provides.gap: Limited multi-dimensional time series support in financial libs
what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: xarray integration for tenor × vintage × time dimensions
what_tidy_signal_provides.benefit: Natural representation of forward curves and term structures

what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: Three-DataFrame pattern (outputs, parameters, metrics) like broom
what_tidy_signal_provides.benefit: Consistent interface for downstream analysis and comparison

what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: Built-in support for seasonality, calendar spreads, crack spreads

what_tidy_signal_provides.gap: Signal validation often manual and inconsistent
what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: Built-in walk-forward analysis, IC analysis, structural break detection
what_tidy_signal_provides.benefit: Rigorous validation workflow baked into package

what_tidy_signal_provides.solution: Immutable frozen dataclass specs with serialization
what_tidy_signal_provides.benefit: Reproducible research, version control for strategies

gap_analysis.unique_value_proposition: Bringing the tidyverse philosophy to commodities signal detection with modern Python tooling, native vintage data support, and comprehensive validation workflows

implementation_recommendations.rationale: Integrated workflow from pair selection through signal generation, leverages OU process optimal stopping theory

implementation_recommendations.scenario: Term structure arbitrage on forward curves
implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: xarray-based term structure modeling with calendar spread detection
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Multi-dimensional data structure naturally handles tenor × time, built-in spread calculations

implementation_recommendations.scenario: Nowcasting commodity prices with ragged-edge data
implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: MIDAS regression with vintage data support
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Handles mixed frequency and publication lags, vintage dimension prevents lookahead bias

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: Vectorized signal generation with vectorbt-style backtesting
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Fast parameter optimization, natural for simple trend rules
Modular signal definitions, easy to weight and combine

controversial_topics: Whether mean reversion works in commodities (conventional wisdom says no, but pairs trading can work)
Optimal lookback windows (vary by market regime)
How much weight to give to out-of-sample vs in-sample performance
Whether to use classification vs regression for signal generation
expert_opinions: Quantopian emphasized: 'Separate alpha factor computation from order execution' (Pipeline API)
Robert Pardo: 'Walk-forward analysis is the gold standard for validation' (1992)
Hadley Wickham: 'Tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way'
Common wisdom: 'Commodities are trending markets, use momentum not mean reversion' - but statistical arbitrage on spreads can work
Best practice: 'Use both ADF and KPSS tests together for robust stationarity assessment'

Pardo, R. (1992). 'Design, Testing and Optimization of Trading Systems.' (Walk-forward analysis)
